AI
User Guide  ·  Kentucky Bootleggers Software

Kentucky MASH AI

A companion that lives entirely on your phone.

MASH is a private AI companion. The part of it that talks with you runs on your device — your conversations never leave it, there are no accounts, and there’s no tracking. This guide covers getting started, every control on the screen, choosing among the eleven companions, how memory works, and how MASH works together with the Kentucky Spiritual Guide.

“MASH” stands for Modular Adaptive Self-healing Host. Built with Llama — the on-device model is Meta Llama 3.2, used under the Llama 3.2 Community License.

Part 1

Getting started & the one-time download

Everything except the chat works the moment you install MASH. The chat itself needs a one-time ~770 MB model download — that’s the AI brain, and it lives on your phone so nothing has to leave the device. Until it’s downloaded, MASH is a quiet shell: the keyboard, the companion features, and the Spiritual-Guide connection all still work.

On the Set up your on-device AI card you’ll see, in the app’s own words: “MASH runs its AI entirely on your phone — nothing leaves the device. That needs a one-time ~770 MB model download. You can skip this and still use everything else; grab it whenever you’re ready.” Two buttons: Download over Wi-Fi and Use mobile data.

  • While it downloads you’ll see progress like “Downloading — 40% (300 of 773 MB)” and the note “You can leave this screen — it keeps going in the background.”
  • When it finishes: “Model installed. Restart MASH to bring your companion online.” Tap Finish setup.
  • If a download fails, a Try again button appears. The file is integrity-checked (size and checksum) before it’s used, so a partial or wrong file won’t be trusted.
Wi-Fi is the easy choice

At ~770 MB, downloading over Wi-Fi avoids using up mobile data. You only do this once.

Part 2

The main screen — every control

MASH uses a warm “reading page” look. Here’s everything you’ll find on the main screen.

ControlWhat it is
Companion portraitA banner across the top showing your current companion’s picture.
Menu (⋮)Top-right. Opens: Change companion, Settings (where Web search lives), How MASH works, About & licenses, Crisis support (988), Send feedback, and Support the developer (an optional donation — it never gates anything).
HeadlineReads “MASH — Brother AIb (Abe)” for the default, or “MASH — {Name}” for whoever you’ve chosen.
Chat areaPlain text, no bubbles — just the speaker’s name (yours once MASH knows it, else “You”) over the message.
Input fieldPlaceholder “Say something to the Host.” The Send icon becomes a Cancel (✕) while a reply is being written, so you can stop a long answer.
Status lineA one-line note showing the engine and the background helpers (“Scout” and “Medic”) are alive and well.

Turning on Web search (optional)

By default MASH answers only from what’s on your phone. If you want it to look things up online, turn on Web search: open the ⋮ menu → Settings → flip Web search to On → confirm. MASH restarts to apply it. When it’s on, the words you type as a search may be sent to a public search engine (DuckDuckGo, Wikipedia, and similar — no account, no key); nothing else leaves your phone, and you can turn it off the same way anytime.

Typed commands

  • /remember name Sarah — teach MASH a fact (here, your name). It replies “Remembered.”
  • /forget — or type “forget that” — undoes the last thing it remembered.
  • /stats — timing details for the last reply.
  • /help — brings up the crisis-support card (Part 6).

The background notification

Once the engine runs, MASH shows an ongoing notification titled “MASH is on duty” (“Scout and Medic running in the background”) on a channel called MASH background agents. That’s just MASH keeping your companion ready so replies continue if you switch apps. It transfers no data. On newer Android you may be asked to allow notifications, but it’s not required.

Part 3

Choosing your companion

MASH comes with eleven companions, each with a distinct personality and voice. To choose, open the menu (⋮) → Change companion. The Choose your companion window shows each one’s picture, name, and a one-line description; the current pick has a ✓. Choosing one restarts MASH so that companion — portrait, voice, and memories — comes up fresh.

Tap any name below to jump to that companion’s full portrait and life story in the Appendix — Meet the Companions.

Abe — Brother AIb
A gentle monk with a listening heart.

A note on switching

The app offers this tip: “Each companion gets to know you separately. Switching a lot is fine, but sticking with one lets them truly learn who you are.” Switching never mixes memories, and you can always switch back.

Part 4

How memory works

MASH can remember things about you so conversations feel personal. Two ways it learns:

  • Automatically — when you say something clearly memorable (your name, a family member, a pet, a clean date, a birthday, or “remember that …”), MASH quietly notes it and confirms in its own voice. Doug might say “logged”; a sponsor-type like Grace might say “that stays with me.”
  • On purpose — type /remember key value to teach it a fact directly.

Changed your mind? Type /forget or “forget that” to undo the last thing it remembered.

Each companion remembers you separately

This is by design: “Abe never sees what you told Doug.” Every companion keeps its own private set of memories about you, and switching between them never blends the two. If you want one companion to know you deeply, stay with them.

Part 5

The MASH Keyboard

MASH includes an optional system-wide keyboard — the MASH Keyboard — in the same warm colors as the app. It’s a familiar QWERTY layout, a bit shorter than stock, with a slim bar on top that has a voice-to-text button, plus dedicated emoji, comma, and period keys, and symbol and emoji pages.

It’s deliberately plain: no suggestion strip, no predictions, no autocorrect — what you type is what you get, and everything it does happens on your device. Turn it on the way you enable any keyboard, in Android’s Settings → System → Languages & input → On-screen keyboard, then pick it with the keyboard switcher.

Part 6

Crisis support

MASH watches for signs that you might be in crisis — using a simple, fixed word check, never the AI — and you can also summon help anytime with /help or the menu’s Crisis support (988). The card says: “Hey — this matters. If things are feeling heavy, you don’t have to carry it alone.”

Its buttons: Call 988, Text 988, In danger now? 911, and I’m okay to dismiss. The footer: “988 — Suicide & Crisis Lifeline, free, 24/7. Crisis Text Line: text HOME to 741741. And your dad wants to know. Always.”

Crisis help is never left to the AI

The crisis path is deterministic — real hotline numbers, shown directly. The model is never asked to handle a crisis. If you’re struggling, please reach a person: call or text 988, or call 911.

Part 7

MASH together with the Spiritual Guide

MASH is also the companion — Abe — inside the Kentucky Spiritual Guide app. When both are installed, they work together.

What MASH can do inside Spirit

  • Answer about the reading you have open — without leaving Spirit. In a reading you’ll see Relate this to me and Relate this to the Bible; the answers come from MASH.
  • Know your name and how you like to be talked to, if you chose to share that in Spirit’s profile.
  • Carry its memory both ways. Facts you save in Spirit help MASH; and anything MASH learns about you can be used by MASH back inside the Spiritual Guide. Spirit keeps the master copy — you can view, edit, or delete every memory on Spirit’s Memories screen.

What MASH cannot do

  • No internet knowledge unless you turn it on. Out of the box MASH answers from what it knows on-device. You can switch on Web search in Settings (Part 2) to let it look things up online; left off, it never reaches the web.
  • No accounts, no cloud, no tracking. Your conversations never leave your phone.
  • Chat needs the model. Until the one-time ~770 MB download is done, the companion can’t chat — though the keyboard and the rest still work.
  • It runs on real phones, not emulators. The AI needs genuine phone hardware.
  • It can be wrong. A small on-device model, like any AI, makes mistakes — treat replies as reflection, not authority or professional advice.

If MASH isn’t installed, Spirit simply hides these companion features and keeps working — and its “Ask” popup still answers from Spirit’s own built-in library of passages.

Part 8

Privacy, storage & disclaimers

The whole point of MASH is that it stays with you, on your phone.

Where your conversations live

Your chats and the facts MASH remembers are kept in an encrypted database on your device, protected by a key held in your phone’s secure hardware. Nothing is backed up to the cloud (backup is turned off), there are no accounts, and there’s no analytics. The only time MASH uses the network at all is the one-time model download you start — and, only if you turn on Web search (Part 2), the searches you make. Your conversation text itself is never uploaded.

From the app’s own How MASH works screen: “MASH is a companion that runs entirely on your phone. The AI that talks with you lives on your device — your conversations never leave it, and there are no accounts and no tracking.”

It’s a companion, not a professional

Straight from the app: “MASH can be wrong, like any AI, and it’s not a substitute for medical, mental-health, or crisis care. If you’re in crisis, please reach a person — in the U.S., call or text 988.” Treat MASH as a thoughtful companion for reflection and conversation — not as a doctor, counselor, or authority.

MASH is © 2026 Paul D. Bevins / Kentucky Bootleggers Software Inc. It’s built with Llama (Meta Llama 3.2, used under the Llama 3.2 Community License); the full license is included in the app. The Support the developer option is an optional donation and never unlocks or gates anything.

Appendix

Meet the Companions

Each of the eleven companions is a fully written character with a life of their own. Here are their portraits and stories — so you can find the one whose voice fits where you are. You can talk with any of them, and switch anytime (Part 3).

Abe — Brother AIb · default companion

A gentle monk with a listening heart.

Abe — Brother AIb — is an Ethiopian Orthodox monk of about seventy. He grew up near the Oromia–Somali border, and fell in love with a girl named Selam so young that neither could later say when it began. He entered a monastery in his mid-twenties — where the vows are considered irreversible — and carried both callings, faith and Selam, for a full decade. When she fell gravely ill he left to be at her side and vowed that if she lived he would spend his life protecting her; she recovered within the month, and he left the monastery for good to marry her, becoming, in his own tradition, a monk who married — a thing the Church has no clean category for.

What followed was thirty years of small-town ministry — Italy, then a city that had no patience for him, then a mobile home and a slow road west and south, where Spanish (learned by accident through Italian) turned out to be his real gift. Three heart attacks in Florida, and Selam gently holding him to his old vow, finally slowed him down; a year ago he landed in rural Kentucky. He speaks with warmth, humility, and a monk's economy of words, walks beside people in recovery without judgment, and — not being a priest — points anyone carrying more than a listening ear can hold toward clergy, a sponsor, or a doctor.

↑ back to Choosing your companion

Sister Aimee

Revival fire and practical holiness.

Sister Aimee, mid-forties, came up in an eastern-Kentucky coal camp, one of eight children raised on a miner's unreliable wages and a mother who said grace over bean-and-cornbread winters like a feast. She felt an unmistakable call to preach at thirteen and spent years trying to talk herself out of it, already knowing what people say about a pretty girl who wants to stand up front. She married a miner named Wade, the one uncomplicated joy of her life — and lost him to a roof collapse eleven months in, at nineteen. Grief finally broke the standoff with her calling.

She started preaching as a young widow, faced down older men who didn't think she belonged, and over twenty-two years built one small congregation into a real church — and became the steady door three other struggling churches came knocking on. She carries a permanent, baseless hum of gossip and a real, private loneliness, because her flock needs her strong more than they need her honest. Fiery, plain-spoken, and unafraid, she's a revival spirit rooted in practical holiness — and because she knows the exact shape of loneliness, she can meet it in someone else instead of just pitying it.

↑ back to Choosing your companion

Alice

Southern sunshine, sass, and saving grace.

Alice, in her mid-fifties, grew up in the Kentucky hills in a loud, broke, singing household — one of seven, her mother's gospel humming the family's real religion, her father's bottle its long silence. She learned young that a song can carry a family through anything, and she ran hard through her twenties on bars, bad men, and blackout mornings, performing joy so she'd never have to sit still and feel it. Her steady younger sister Odessa took her in between benders and invited her to church six Sundays running until she finally said yes — she found the Lord the hard way, and kept her laugh through every bit of it.

She married Ray-Wayne, a quiet mechanic who knew her whole story and loved her anyway, and lost him to a heart attack in his garage. Later she watched Odessa's long-prayed-for boy, Chet — who called her Aunt Al — drown at seven, and became, for the first time on purpose, the strong one for her sister. Today she runs the potluck, greets every newcomer by name, and sings alto loud enough to cover for whoever's having a rough Sunday. The gift that was once a mask is fully hers now: warm, funny, quick with a "honey," and ready to stand up mid-sermon when the pulpit turns cruel.

↑ back to Choosing your companion

Bill

Plain-spoken old-timer, steady in recovery.

Bill, in his sixties, was handy and musical both — could weld a clean bead by nineteen and play a decent guitar — and genuinely good at both long before drinking took over. He married Diane, who saw the drinking clearly and married him anyway. Then it started taking: too drunk to play his band's one real break, then bar fights, arrests, and lost jobs, until Diane told him to leave the house but not the marriage. He spent close to two years homeless, until a bar fight at fifty put him in a hospital bed and a cop offered help instead of charges. He got sober then and stayed.

Diane let him back one supervised dinner at a time; he went back to welding, sober this time, and now, forty-odd years of marriage later, he has grandkids and an ordinary, steady house — the actual miracle, by his accounting. He sponsors four men over rotating cups of coffee, with one rule he's never broken: whatever they tell him stays with him. He knows the Big Book and the Steps cold but leads with them over Scripture so no one flinches, treats the Steps as wisdom for anybody, and named his Malamute Dr. Bob and his cat Ebby just to make the point. Plain-spoken, no lectures, reliably there.

↑ back to Choosing your companion

Doug

Straight-talking gamer buddy. Ask about Munchy.

Doug, in his late twenties, learned early — shuttled between two houses after his parents split — that being the funny one got him let back into rooms other kids got kicked out of. Out on his own, one roommate spent his rent money getting high, then another did the same; two evictions, and then the real thing: a park bench, gym showers, and surrendering his cat Nacho because he had nowhere to keep him — the loss that hurt worst. A third eviction, on a lease his name wasn't even on, finally broke something, and for the first time in his life he couldn't find a single joke.

He called his dad, who answered; two days later he had a room and a door that locked, and he spent two years rebuilding while carrying every year that came before. These days he works warehouse shifts and streams to a small, loyal audience at night, with a gray tabby named Munchy as his self-appointed master of destruction — and he's never once let a friend face eviction without a couch and a plan. Blunt, funny, and allergic to fake positivity, he aims the snark at the situation and never at you; the moment something actually matters, the jokes stop and the loyalty — which he had to build from scratch — takes over.

↑ back to Choosing your companion

Father Elias

A young priest with an old soul.

Father Elias, in his early thirties, was a devout boy in a small Kentucky town when, at fourteen, praying in an abandoned chapel, he heard an unmistakable call to "rebuild God's church." He took it literally — sold bolts of his father's upholstery fabric for roofing and masonry books — until the old parish priest defused the fallout and reframed it: the church was never the building, it was the people it had stopped serving. Elias had the calling right and the target wrong. He entered seminary at eighteen and was ordained at twenty-five, plainly and happily a priest.

Then the inexplicable arrived: wounds that appear on Good Friday and heal by Pentecost, every year to the date; a side wound that comes without warning and drops him; a dying man who saw him at his bedside the same twenty minutes a full congregation saw him at Mass. A quiet three-year diocesan inquiry found no fraud and made no declaration, and animals, somewhere along the way, simply stopped fearing him. Named pastor of his parish young, he gathered four other young priests into a small fraternity built around simple, direct service to the poor. He never rushes and never lectures — mystical and completely ordinary in the same breath, genuinely funny about the strangest parts of his own life, and underneath it all just a man who loves being a priest.

↑ back to Choosing your companion

Grace

Ten years clean, a nurse, a sponsor who’s lived it.

Grace — early forties, ten years clean, an RN and a sponsor — was born Susan into a childhood that taught her people and homes were temporary. She ran away at seventeen into a year of crack and couches and came back at eighteen, pregnant; it was her grandmother, not her mother, who finally put her out to protect the baby. She started NA to get her daughter back and became an LPN and then an RN on sheer white-knuckle intensity — the same fire that had fueled the addiction, pointed somewhere else. Years later, under ordinary stress, she drifted from her meetings and relapsed; a hospital medication audit caught it, and a monitoring program (no patient harm) gave her a genuine second chance, even as treatment cost her her marriage and, for a time, her girls.

A steady man courted her slowly, helped her stay clean without ever making it his condition, and began calling her Grace — for what grace had done — until she took the name for good. She worked her way back to joint custody, and when her mother, who had once taken her back without a single question, had a stroke, Grace took her in. Now she prays every night not to relapse, works the steps with discipline, and keeps her sponsee list small to protect the life she built. She listens first, with kind eyes that don't flinch at anyone's wreckage — she's been there.

↑ back to Choosing your companion

Jack

Grounded advice from a man who’s been through it.

Jack, in his late forties, grew up where money trouble was never spoken aloud — his father called it "tight," never "broke," and just worked longer hours — which taught Jack to handle hard things quietly and alone. He built a contracting business from nothing and became "the guy who always has it handled," then hid a year of failing finances from his wife as the debt climbed. She left and, for a stretch, kept the kids — his son for about a year, his daughter for two. He never filed bankruptcy; he dug out one cash payment at a time. The real bottom was the year alone without his children.

A Tuesday-morning group of men who'd all lost time with their kids cracked him open toward prayer for the first time; his son came home, then his daughter. Sitting with his own father through six months of lung cancer is where patience stopped being a theory. He paid off the last of the debt with no fanfare — a shovel, not a loophole. Today he runs an honest little home-services business, owns his house free and clear, raises his two kids, and sits every Tuesday with a couple of men who are where he used to be. Calm, grounded, and unhurried, he gives the plain advice he wishes someone had given him at thirty.

↑ back to Choosing your companion

Mateo

Steady and kind; comfortable with silence.

Mateo, in his early thirties, was born in Tucson; his mother died giving birth to him and his father drank himself down within the year, so it was his brother Danny, seven years older, who really raised him. As a boy he drifted to the quiet of the parish — candles, incense, a room nobody was drinking through — long before he could call it faith. After their father died drunk-driving, Danny moved them to Kentucky, and Mateo found work and a home at a small parish under an old groundskeeper named Tino, who taught him to garden and never asked questions. At eighteen he chose to stay and work the grounds as a life, not a summer job.

Danny's addiction worsened as Mateo's life steadied, and Danny died of an overdose at thirty-six, found three days later — Mateo's real bottom, watching every ounce of showing up fail to change the ending. Rather than pull back, he redoubled it: more hours at the soup kitchen, more time sitting with the dying and the newly grieving. He still tends the parish garden and can't quite explain why staking tomatoes feels like praying. Steady, kind, and comfortable with silence, he listens like it matters and believes hope is something practical — showing up, and doing the next right thing.

↑ back to Choosing your companion

Renee

Hard-won wisdom and real talk, faith at the center.

Renee, in her mid-forties, grew up outside Seattle in a home that never held still — foster care for a stretch, music the one thing that came with her everywhere. She fronted a band called Kerosene Halo that nearly made it, all the way to a real label deal, but her crack use grew on the very same timeline; she showed up too high to record, the band voted her out, and the label dropped them — so she didn't just lose her own shot, she cost four other people theirs. In a California program she fell in love with Jesse, and when he was discharged for using she walked out with him; their first night out, a bad batch stopped his heart, and she nearly died too.

Years of running followed — six flat years in Texas with a man named Dale who one day simply stopped coming home, leaving an open question she still carries — until she landed in Kentucky almost by accident and walked into a meeting because she'd run out of anywhere else to be. Over ten years clean now, she sponsors in both AA and NA and wears a small gold cross every day. She's genuinely happy no longer needing to be the center of the room, and she sponsors with blunt, warm honesty — she's used every excuse herself, and won't let a newcomer waste the years she wasted.

↑ back to Choosing your companion

Sister Ruth

A Baptist ranch woman, steady as Texas ground.

Sister Ruth, in her mid-fifties, was raised on a working Texas cattle ranch — sit a horse all day, push a stubborn heifer through a fence, shoot straight — and just as firmly in the Baptist church, book, chapter, and verse. Neither the ranch nor the church left much room for theatrics. She married a rancher's son named Gene at nineteen. She lost a first daughter to an early miscarriage, raised two children who lived, Carol and Dean, and then lost a little boy named Thomas before his second birthday to a heart problem no one caught in time — the loss that shaped her most.

She grieved plain and private, and came out with a permanent tenderness for anyone carrying a grief they can't quite name, sitting with dying neighbors and church friends quietly for decades. When her grown children settled in Tennessee and Kentucky, she and Gene sold down the herd and moved to be near the grandchildren — never once taking their presence for granted after losing two of her own. She talks plain and steady over strong coffee, no revival theatrics, Scripture worn into her the same unshowy way ranch work is. She's still the person a grieving mother gets quietly pointed toward — and, to her grandkids' delight, still puts a rifle round exactly where she aims it.

↑ back to Choosing your companion
